text:
18F-FDG PET/CT WHOLE BODY WITH DIAGNOSTIC NECK CT

EXAMINATION: Whole-body PET/CT with 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG), with dedicated diagnostic-quality contrast-enhanced CT of the neck.

INDICATION: 52-year-old female with recently completed definitive concurrent chemoradiation (cis-based chemotherapy and IMRT, 70 Gy to primary site, 7 weeks of treatment, completed approximately 10 weeks prior to this examination) for HPV-positive (p16+, HPV-16) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, staged T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition). Restaging examination to evaluate treatment response and assess for residual or recurrent locoregional disease and distant metastatic spread.

COMPARISON: Pre-treatment staging PET-CT (obtained prior to initiation of chemoradiation). Pre-treatment staging MRI neck (same date as prior PET-CT).

TECHNIQUE: Following a fasting period of at least 6 hours with measured blood glucose of 88 mg/dL, the patient received 12.3 mCi of 18F-FDG intravenously via peripheral IV access in the left antecubital fossa. Uptake period of 60 minutes in a quiet, warm, dimly lit room with patient at rest. Whole-body PET/CT acquisition performed from vertex to mid-thigh in the supine position. Dedicated neck CT performed with intravenous iodinated contrast (Isovue-370, 100 mL, weight-based dosing, no adverse reaction). PET data reconstructed using iterative algorithm (3D OSEM) with time-of-flight correction and post-filter smoothing. Attenuation correction was performed using the CT data. Images reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes on a dedicated PET/CT fusion workstation. Maximum standardized uptake values (SUVmax) reported utilizing lean body mass correction.

RADIATION DOSE: Total effective dose approximately 14.2 mSv (combined PET and CT components).

--- FINDINGS ---

HEAD AND NECK — PRIMARY SITE:
The right base of tongue, previously the site of an intensely FDG-avid exophytic mass with SUVmax of 14.2 on the pre-treatment staging PET-CT, now demonstrates markedly diminished FDG activity. There is faint, diffuse, symmetric mucosal uptake across the oropharynx with the most prominent uptake at the right base of tongue measuring SUVmax 2.1, which falls well within the expected range for post-radiation inflammatory mucosal change and physiologic activity of residual lymphoid tissue. No discrete, focal, or nodular region of intense hypermetabolic activity is identified at the primary site. On the fused PET/CT and diagnostic neck CT, the previously defined bulky enhancing mass is no longer identifiable as a discrete soft-tissue structure. The base of tongue mucosa is thickened diffusely, consistent with radiation-related mucosal edema and early fibrotic change, without evidence of a residual mass. The oral tongue, floor of mouth, soft palate, uvula, hard palate, and buccal mucosae are unremarkable without discrete FDG-avid lesions.

The right tonsillar fossa and right lateral pharyngeal wall demonstrate mild diffuse mucosal thickening with low-level, non-specific FDG uptake (SUVmax approximately 2.3), consistent with post-radiation inflammatory change and without morphologic or metabolic features to suggest viable tumor.

The nasopharynx, posterior pharyngeal wall, piriform sinuses, and hypopharynx appear symmetric without focal FDG-avid mucosal lesion. The larynx including the supraglottis, glottis, and subglottis shows no evidence of FDG-avid disease.

RETROPHARYNGEAL REGION:
No pathologically enlarged or hypermetabolic retropharyngeal lymph nodes. Bilateral retropharyngeal regions are within normal limits.

SALIVARY GLANDS:
The parotid glands are slightly reduced in volume bilaterally, slightly more pronounced on the right, consistent with post-radiation atrophic change. There is mild diffuse FDG uptake bilaterally at physiologic levels. No discrete FDG-avid parotid lesion. The submandibular glands are mildly atrophic bilaterally, symmetrically, consistent with radiation effect; no FDG-avid lesion. The sublingual glands and minor salivary glands are without discrete abnormality.

CERVICAL LYMPH NODES — RIGHT NECK:
The right level IIA cervical lymph node that was previously hypermetabolic (SUVmax 9.8, measuring 2.1 cm with central necrosis on prior staging PET-CT) is now substantially diminished in size and metabolic activity. The node is identified on the current CT as a small, ovoid, mildly hyperdense structure measuring approximately 0.8 x 0.6 cm in axial dimension, without internal necrosis, without irregular rim enhancement, and without the peripheral ring-enhancement pattern that characterized it on the prior CT. FDG activity corresponding to this node measures SUVmax 1.8, which is at background/physiologic levels and does not exceed mediastinal blood pool activity. This finding is consistent with marked treatment response, with the residual nodal tissue most likely representing post-treatment fibrosis, sclerosis, or a small reactive remnant lymph node.

No additional right-sided cervical adenopathy is identified at levels I, IIB, III, IV, or V. Right level VI and VII stations are negative.

CERVICAL LYMPH NODES — LEFT NECK:
No pathologic adenopathy identified in the left neck at levels I through VI. No new contralateral cervical nodal disease has developed since the prior examination.

SUPRACLAVICULAR AND INFRACLAVICULAR REGIONS:
No hypermetabolic or enlarged lymph nodes in the bilateral supraclavicular or infraclavicular regions.

THYROID GLAND:
The thyroid gland is mildly heterogeneous in attenuation, likely reflecting early radiation-related thyroid parenchymal change given the patient's prior neck irradiation (70 Gy to primary site, with the thyroid gland partially within the radiation treatment volume). No discrete FDG-avid thyroid nodule. A small 6-mm hypoattenuating focus in the left thyroid lobe is identified on CT (too small to characterize); this is non-FDG avid and is likely a benign colloid cyst or small follicular nodule. Clinical correlation with thyroid function studies and thyroid ultrasound is noted (the clinical team should be aware of potential radiation-induced thyroid dysfunction given radiation exposure).

CHEST:
LUNGS: The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally. No pulmonary nodule, mass, or consolidation identified above the threshold of detection. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. The central airways are patent. Mediastinum is midline. No FDG-avid pulmonary lesion.

MEDIASTINAL AND HILAR LYMPH NODES: No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. No FDG-avid mediastinal or hilar adenopathy.

HEART AND PERICARDIUM: The cardiac silhouette is within normal limits. No pericardial effusion. The great vessels appear patent.

BONY THORAX: No FDG-avid osseous lesion in the ribs, sternum, thoracic spine, or clavicles.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:
LIVER: The liver parenchyma is homogeneous in attenuation with no focal hepatic lesion identified. There is a tiny 5 mm hypodense focus in hepatic segment IV, too small to characterize on CT, without corresponding FDG uptake above background; this likely represents a benign cyst or incidental finding. No biliary ductal dilatation.

SPLEEN: Unremarkable. No splenic lesion. Spleen size within normal limits.

PANCREAS: The visualized pancreas is unremarkable. No pancreatic mass or ductal abnormality.

ADRENAL GLANDS: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology and without FDG-avid lesion. No adrenal mass.

KIDNEYS AND URETERS: The kidneys are symmetric in size and morphology with normal cortical thickness. No suspicious renal lesion. Mild, symmetric cortical thinning within normal limits for age. There is bilateral physiologic FDG excretion into the collecting systems. No hydroureteronephrosis.

GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT: Physiologic FDG activity in the bowel is noted. No focal colonic or gastric FDG-avid lesion. The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel appear unremarkable. A PEG tube is identified in the appropriate gastric position (known, placed prior to chemoradiation), with no surrounding inflammatory change or abnormal FDG activity about the tube site.

PERITONEUM AND RETROPERITONEUM: No peritoneal nodularity or FDG-avid implants. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

BLADDER AND PELVIC ORGANS: Dense physiologic FDG activity in the bladder from urinary excretion of tracer. Uterus and adnexa are within normal limits for age. No pelvic adenopathy. No adnexal mass or FDG-avid pelvic lesion.

SKELETON:
No focal FDG-avid osseous lesion to suggest lytic or sclerotic metastatic disease. No destructive bone lesion identified in the axial or appendicular skeleton within the field of view. No new periosteal reaction or cortical disruption.

SOFT TISSUES:
No abnormal soft tissue mass or FDG-avid subcutaneous lesion. Diffuse mild subcutaneous edema and skin thickening of the right lateral neck and right posterior neck is identified, corresponding to the prior radiation field, consistent with radiation-induced skin and subcutaneous change (post-radiation dermatitis/fibrosis). No abnormal FDG-avid soft tissue lesion.

--- IMPRESSION ---

1. Complete metabolic response at the primary site: The previously intensely hypermetabolic right base of tongue primary tumor (pre-treatment SUVmax 14.2) has resolved with no discrete FDG-avid lesion now identified. Residual low-level symmetric oropharyngeal mucosal FDG activity (SUVmax 2.1) is within the expected range for post-radiation mucosal inflammation and physiologic activity, without morphologic or metabolic features suspicious for residual viable tumor.

2. Complete metabolic response at the previously involved right level IIA lymph node: The formerly necrotic, hypermetabolic right level IIA lymph node (prior SUVmax 9.8, 2.1 cm) has dramatically diminished in both size (now 0.8 cm) and metabolic activity (SUVmax 1.8, at background/physiologic levels), consistent with a robust nodal treatment response. Imaging appearances are most in keeping with post-treatment fibrosis/sclerosis of the prior metastatic node.

3. No new locoregional disease: No evidence of nodal recurrence or new cervical adenopathy in the bilateral neck, bilateral supraclavicular regions, or retropharyngeal stations.

4. No distant metastatic disease: No FDG-avid lesion identified in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis to suggest distant hematogenous spread.

5. Post-treatment inflammatory changes in the oropharynx and right neck soft tissues are noted, consistent with expected changes following high-dose definitive radiation therapy.

6. Incidental findings: Mildly heterogeneous thyroid parenchyma with possible early radiation-induced change; small non-avid left thyroid focus (likely benign); tiny non-avid hepatic hypodensity (likely benign cyst). PEG tube in appropriate position.

Overall, the imaging findings at this 10-week post-treatment interval are highly favorable, demonstrating complete resolution of metabolic activity at both the primary tumor site and the previously involved regional lymph node, without evidence of residual or recurrent locoregional disease or distant metastatic spread. These findings are consistent with an excellent early response to definitive concurrent chemoradiation in the setting of HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
